name: merge
context: Combine branches using fast-forward, three-way merge, squash merge, and resolve merge conflicts
info: Merging integrates changes from one branch into another. Git supports several merge strategies: fast-forward (linear history when possible), three-way merge (creates a merge commit when branches have diverged), and squash merge (condenses a branch into a single commit). This skill covers executing merges, understanding merge strategies, resolving conflicts when files are changed in both branches, aborting failed merges, and choosing between merge and rebase workflows. Agents must understand merge conflicts — they occur when the same region of a file was modified differently in the two branches. Conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>) must be resolved manually or programmatically. See [[git/rebase]] for the alternative history-rewriting integration method, and [[git/branch]] for branch management.
body:
# Git Merge

Merging brings divergent lines of development back together. It's the primary way to integrate feature work into the main branch.

## Merge Strategies

### Fast-Forward Merge

When the target branch hasn't diverged, git just moves the pointer forward. No merge commit is created.

```bash
# Fast-forward if possible (default)
git checkout main
git merge feature-branch
```

```
Before:  main ── A ── B
                       \
         feature ────── C ── D

After:   main ── A ── B ── C ── D
```

### No Fast-Forward Merge

Force a merge commit even when fast-forward is possible. Preserves branch topology.

```bash
git merge --no-ff feature-branch
```

```
Before:  main ── A ── B
                       \
         feature ────── C ── D

After:   main ── A ── B ──────── M
                       \        /
         feature ────── C ── D
```

### Three-Way Merge

When both branches have new commits, git finds the merge base and creates a merge commit automatically.

```bash
git checkout main
git merge feature-branch
# Git creates a merge commit combining both histories
```

### Squash Merge

Condenses all commits from a branch into a single set of changes staged in the index. You then commit manually.

```bash
git checkout main
git merge --squash feature-branch
git commit -m "feat(auth): add OAuth2 support (#42)"
```

Squash merges produce cleaner history but lose individual commit detail. The source branch appears unmerged to git (since no merge commit links them), so delete it after squash merging.

## Resolving Merge Conflicts

Conflicts occur when both branches modify the same region of a file.

```bash
# Start the merge
git merge feature-branch
# CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in src/auth.js
```

### Conflict Markers

```javascript
<<<<<<< HEAD
const timeout = 5000;
=======
const timeout = 10000;
>>>>>>> feature-branch
```

- `<<<<<<< HEAD` — current branch version
- `=======` — separator
- `>>>>>>> feature-branch` — incoming branch version

### Resolution Steps

1. Open each conflicted file
2. Choose the correct code (or combine both)
3. Remove all conflict markers
4. Stage resolved files: `git add src/auth.js`
5. Complete the merge: `git commit`

```bash
# See which files are conflicted
git status

# Use theirs (accept incoming) for a specific file
git checkout --theirs src/auth.js

# Use ours (keep current) for a specific file
git checkout --ours src/auth.js

# After resolving, stage and commit
git add src/auth.js
git commit
```

### Aborting a Merge

```bash
# Abort and return to pre-merge state
git merge --abort
```

## Merge Options

```bash
# Merge with a custom message
git merge feature-branch -m "Merge feature-branch: add OAuth2"

# Merge but don't auto-commit (inspect first)
git merge --no-commit feature-branch

# Merge with specific strategy
git merge -s recursive feature-branch
git merge -s ours feature-branch  # keep our version entirely

# Merge with strategy option
git merge -X theirs feature-branch  # auto-resolve conflicts favoring theirs
git merge -X ours feature-branch    # auto-resolve conflicts favoring ours
```

## Verifying a Merge

```bash
# Check the merge result
git log --oneline --graph -10

# Verify no unresolved conflicts
git diff --check

# See what the merge brought in
git diff HEAD~1..HEAD
```

## Agent Best Practices

1. **Always pull before merging** — `git pull origin main` ensures you have the latest
2. **Prefer --no-ff for feature merges** — preserves branch history in the graph
3. **Review conflicts carefully** — don't blindly accept --ours or --theirs
4. **Test after merging** — run the test suite to catch integration issues
5. **Delete merged branches** — `git branch -d feature-branch` keeps things clean
6. **Use squash for small PRs** — keeps main history clean

sections.title: Fast-forward merge
sections.body: When the target branch has no new commits since the source branched off, git simply moves the target pointer forward. No merge commit is created. This is the default behavior. Use `--no-ff` to force a merge commit anyway, which preserves the fact that a branch existed. Fast-forward is clean but loses branch topology information.

sections.title: Three-way merge
sections.body: When both branches have new commits, git computes the merge base (the common ancestor) and performs a three-way merge between base, ours, and theirs. If there are no conflicts, git auto-creates a merge commit with two parents. The merge commit preserves the full history of both branches. Use `git log --graph` to visualize the branch/merge topology.

sections.title: Squash merge
sections.body: Squash merge (`git merge --squash`) applies all changes from the source branch as staged modifications without creating a merge commit. You then create a normal (single-parent) commit. This produces linear history but loses individual commits. Important: git doesn't record the merge relationship, so the source branch appears unmerged — always delete it after squash merging to avoid confusion.

sections.title: Resolving merge conflicts
sections.body: Conflicts occur when the same file region is modified differently in both branches. Git inserts conflict markers (`<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`) into the file. Resolution: edit the file to keep the correct code, remove markers, `git add` the resolved file, then `git commit`. Shortcuts: `git checkout --ours <file>` keeps the current branch version, `git checkout --theirs <file>` accepts the incoming version. Use `-X theirs` or `-X ours` as merge strategy options to auto-resolve all conflicts one way. See [[git/troubleshooting]] for complex conflict scenarios.

sections.title: Aborting a merge
sections.body: If a merge goes wrong or you want to start over, run `git merge --abort` to return to the pre-merge state. This resets HEAD, the index, and the working tree to the state before `git merge` was called. Only works during an unfinished merge (when `.git/MERGE_HEAD` exists).

links.context: Alternative to merge for integrating changes
Merge creates a commit (or you commit after squash)

related.note: Rebase rewrites history instead of creating merge commits
Branches must exist before they can be merged
Merges create commits; squash merges require a manual commit

scripts.name: Safe Merge
scripts.description: Merge a branch with conflict detection and dry-run option
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

SOURCE="${1:?Usage: safe-merge.sh <source-branch> [--squash] [--no-ff] [--dry-run]}"
shift

SQUASH=""
NOFF=""
DRYRUN=false

for arg in "$@"; do
  case "$arg" in
    --squash) SQUASH="--squash" ;;
    --no-ff) NOFF="--no-ff" ;;
    --dry-run) DRYRUN=true ;;
  esac
done

CURRENT=$(git branch --show-current)
echo "Merging $SOURCE into $CURRENT"

# Check if source branch exists
if ! git rev-parse --verify "$SOURCE" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "Error: Branch '$SOURCE' does not exist"
  exit 1
fi

# Dry run — check for conflicts without merging
if $DRYRUN; then
  echo "=== Dry run: checking for conflicts ==="
  if git merge --no-commit --no-ff "$SOURCE" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "No conflicts detected. Merge would succeed."
    git merge --abort 2>/dev/null || true
  else
    echo "Conflicts detected in:"
    git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U
    git merge --abort
  fi
  exit 0
fi

# Perform the merge
git merge $SQUASH $NOFF "$SOURCE"

if [ -n "$SQUASH" ]; then
  echo "Squash merge staged. Review and commit:"
  git diff --cached --stat
else
  echo "Merge complete:"
  git log --oneline --graph -5
fi

depends.reason: Source and target branches must exist

author: dojo-community
